coredata.dc:description: Programmed cell death 1 ligand 1 (PD-L1) immunotherapy holds a pivotal role in lung cancer treatment. However, current methods for monitoring PD-L1 expression exhibit several limitations, including hysteresis and the invasive nature of tissue sampling. Circulating tumor cells (CTCs), the important biomarkers in liquid biopsy, are minimally invasive and facilitate continuous monitoring. Consequently, integrating CTC counting with PD-L1 expression analysis offers more comprehensive insights for the development of personalized treatment strategies development and efficacy evaluations. In this study, we presented a facile liquid biopsy assay designed for the dynamic monitoring of PD-L1 expression on CTCs captured from non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) patients. This assay was achieved by fabricating two high-performance probes: EpCAM and Vimentin dual-aptamer modified nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots probe (E/V-apt-N-CQDs) and hairpin PD-L1 aptamer coupled with gold nanoparticles (PD-L1-apt-AuNPs). The E/V-apt-N-CQDs probe effectively captured two types of CTC models (H1299 and A549) exhibiting differential PD-L1 expression. Additionally, reagent-less detection of PD-L1 levels on CTCs was achieved using a portable magnetic electrochemical sensor with excellent specificity and sensitivity, which was capable of measuring PD-L1 concentrations as low as 2 ng/mL. Finally, this assay was applied in 41 NSCLC patients to investigate the correlation between CTC numbers or PD-L1 expression and disease progression and immunotherapy efficacy. The results indicated a significant association between elevated CTC counts or reduced PD-L1 levels and clinical progression. Moreover, this liquid assay successfully monitored dynamic changes in CTCs and PD-L1 expression in NSCLC patients receiving immunotherapy, indicating its potential for clinical application.

(C) EpCAM, CSV, and PD-L1 expressions on H1299, A549, THP-1, and PBMC by western blot analysis. Densitometric analysis of the blots (right). (D) Real-time PCR analysis of mRNA expression in H1299, A549, and THP-1 cells. ns: not significant; ∗P < 0.05 and ∗∗P < 0.01.

(C) Enrichment efficiencies of E/C-apt-N-CQDs probe for H1299, A549, THP-1, and PBMC. (D) Comparison of capture ability toward H1299, A549, and THP-1 cells among N-CQDs, E-apt-N-CQDs, C-apt-N-CQDs, and E/C-apt-N-CQDs probes. Cell number: 200 cells/mL. (E) Capture efficiencies of E/C-apt-N-CQDs toward different numbers of H1299 cells in PBS. (F) Capture performances and (G) linear correlation analysis of E/C-apt-N-CQDs toward H1299 cells in PBS (pH 7.4), THP-1 cell mixture (106 cells/mL), and blood. ns: not significant; ∗∗P < 0.01. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) CV curves of bare SPCE (a), Fe3O4/SPCE (b), glutaraldehyde-Fe3O4/SPCE (c), and Fe3O4-SA/SPCE (d) sensors in 1 mM K3Fe(CN)6 solution. (B) SWV curves obtained on bare SPCE, Fe3O4-SA/SPCE, and PD-L1-apt-AuNPs/Fe3O4-SA/SPCE toward 10 ng/mL PD-L1. SWV responses toward different concentrations of PD-L1 recombinant proteins (C) and the corresponding linear regression curve (D). (E) Electrochemical responses of 6 ng/mL concentrations of PD-L1, EpCAM, Vimentin, E-cadherin, N-cadherin, and P-cadherin when incubated with the PD-L1-apt-AuNPs probe, respectively. (F–H) SWV curves of the Fe3O4-SA/SPCE sensor toward different numbers of H1299 (F), A549 (G), and two linear regression curves (H). (I) Comparison of SWV responses obtained on THP-1 (500 cells/mL) and H1299 cells (100 cells/mL).

(A) Electrochemical curve corresponding to PD-L1 expression in CTCs obtained from patient #8. (B) ICC staining of the captured CTCs from patient #8. Correlations between PD-L1 expression and gender (C), age (D), clinical subtypes (E), clinical stages (F), lymph node metastasis (G), and distant metastasis (H) in NSCLC patients. Changes in CTC numbers (I) and PD-L1 expression (J) before and after ICIs treatment. ns: not significant; ∗P < 0.05.

head.ce:title: A facile liquid biopsy assay for highly efficient CTCs capture and reagent-less monitoring of immune checkpoint PD-L1 expression on CTCs with non-small cell lung cancer patients

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Programmed cell death 1 ligand 1 (PD-L1) immunotherapy holds a pivotal role in lung cancer treatment. However, current methods for monitoring PD-L1 expression exhibit several limitations, including hysteresis and the invasive nature of tissue sampling. Circulating tumor cells (CTCs), the important biomarkers in liquid biopsy, are minimally invasive and facilitate continuous monitoring. Consequently, integrating CTC counting with PD-L1 expression analysis offers more comprehensive insights for the development of personalized treatment strategies development and efficacy evaluations. In this study, we presented a facile liquid biopsy assay designed for the dynamic monitoring of PD-L1 expression on CTCs captured from non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) patients. This assay was achieved by fabricating two high-performance probes: EpCAM and Vimentin dual-aptamer modified nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots probe (E/V-apt-N-CQDs) and hairpin PD-L1 aptamer coupled with gold nanoparticles (PD-L1-apt-AuNPs). The E/V-apt-N-CQDs probe effectively captured two types of CTC models (H1299 and A549) exhibiting differential PD-L1 expression. Additionally, reagent-less detection of PD-L1 levels on CTCs was achieved using a portable magnetic electrochemical sensor with excellent specificity and sensitivity, which was capable of measuring PD-L1 concentrations as low as 2 ng/mL. Finally, this assay was applied in 41 NSCLC patients to investigate the correlation between CTC numbers or PD-L1 expression and disease progression and immunotherapy efficacy. The results indicated a significant association between elevated CTC counts or reduced PD-L1 levels and clinical progression. Moreover, this liquid assay successfully monitored dynamic changes in CTCs and PD-L1 expression in NSCLC patients receiving immunotherapy, indicating its potential for clinical application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), which accounts for 80–85% of lung cancer cases, is a leading cause of cancer-related mortality among both men and women (Li et al., 2024). Current treatments for lung cancer primarily include surgical resection, neoadjuvant chemotherapy, and targeted therapy. The emergence of immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) has introduced new therapeutic possibilities for lung cancer patients, especially those with metastatic NSCLC. These agents may be employed either as monotherapy or in combination with chemotherapy or anti-angiogenic antibodies, thereby expanding the standard treatment options for these patients (Di Federico et al., 2024).

Two types of ICIs, namely programmed cell death protein 1 (PD-1) or its ligand PD-L1 inhibitors, and cytotoxic T-lymphocyte-associated protein 4 inhibitors, have been approved by the FDA (Mao et al., 2022). Tumor PD-L1 expression serves as one of the most mature predictive biomarkers for the response to anti-PD-1/PD-L1 therapy and is the only predictive biomarker currently used in clinical practice (Holder et al., 2024). The diagnosis and treatment guidelines specify that patients with PD-L1 positivity (tumor proportion score (TPS) ≥ 1%) are eligible for pembrolizumab (anti-PD-1 antibody) therapy, with those exhibiting high PD-L1 expression (TPS ≥50%) showing even greater benefits from immunotherapy (Hanna et al., 2020; Jaiyesimi et al., 2024). PD-L1 expression can be detected using various methods, including immunohistochemistry (IHC) on tissue samples (Cheng et al., 2020), immunocytochemistry (ICC) on cell samples (Protsepko et al., 2023), flow cytometry (Serrati et al., 2023), and fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH) (Miao et al., 2023). Among these detection techniques, IHC is recognized as the most cost-effective and widely used method. Nevertheless, studies indicate that over 50% of patients with high PD-L1 expression do not benefit from first-line pembrolizumab treatment (O'Brien et al). Conversely, approximately 10% of PD-L1 negative patients demonstrate a positive response to second-line anti-PD-1/PD-L1 therapy (Brastianos et al., 2022). Possible explanations for this discrepancy may include the lack of standardized evaluation methods for IHC, tumor heterogeneity and evolution, and dynamic changes in PD-L1 expression during treatment (Chen et al., 2023; Hao et al., 2023). Therefore, to better assess the benefits of ICIs therapy, it is necessary to conduct companion tests for PD-L1 expression in cancer patients. However, the necessity for repeated biopsies poses challenges, as it may increase patient trauma and complications, making it difficult to implement in clinical settings.

In contrast to traditional tissue biopsy, liquid biopsy offers several advantages in tumor assessment, including minimal invasiveness, synergy, rapid execution, and the ability to dynamically analyze molecules or cells, thereby providing significant potential for early diagnosis, progression monitoring, and therapeutic effect evaluations (Bu et al., 2022; Kwizera et al., 2022; Lu et al., 2024; Moro et al., 2023). Recent studies have highlighted the exceptional potential of liquid biopsy in evaluating responses to immunotherapy through the detection of PD-L1 expression and tumor mutational burden (Yang et al., 2023). For instance, PD-L1 expression in tumor-derived exosomes was detected with high specificity and sensitivity using established aptasensors that employ parallel rolling circle amplification (He et al., 2024; Zeng et al., 2024). Moreover, Circulating tumor cells (CTCs) are tumor cells that detach from the primary tumor and enter the peripheral blood, lymphatic vessels, and other pathways, which can migrate to distant sites to form metastasis (Liu et al., 2024; Sun et al., 2024; Zhuang et al., 2022). The enumeration of CTCs in patient bloods has been correlated with a high risk of tumor metastasis and a reduced time to relapse, making CTCs an attractive marker for cancer research (Ahmed et al., 2024). Moreover, research has demonstrated that the expression level of PD-L1 on CTCs in peripheral blood is consistent with that on tumor tissue, indicating that PD-L1 expression on CTCs can serve as a reliable indicator for detection (Gong et al., 2022; Saputra et al., 2024). Therefore, due to the advantage of CTCs as a liquid biopsy marker that can be repeatedly sampled and dynamically monitored, the analysis of PD-L1 expression on CTCs, as well as CTCs enumeration, holds great promise for guiding personalized immunotherapy.

Due to the extremely low prevalence of CTCs in the blood (with only 1 CTC per 107 to 109 blood cells) (Boya et al., 2022), current CTCs capture strategies that rely on epithelial cell adhesion molecule (EpCAM) as the sole separation target often yield inadequate sensitivity and specificity. This limitation arises from the occurrence of epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT) (Liu et al., 2022; Xia et al., 2021), during which cells with high metastatic potential exhibit downregulation of epithelial markers (e.g., EpCAM), rendering key subpopulations of CTCs undetectable by epithelial marker-based methods (Yang et al., 2021; Zhu et al., 2020). In previous studies, our group incorporated interstitial marker (e.g., Vimentin, PTK7) alongside epithelial marker (e.g., EpCAM) for CTC capture and detection, significantly enhancing the efficiency and purity of captured CTCs in blood samples (Li et al., 2022a, 2022b; Zuo et al., 2023). The application of EpCAM aptamer (SYL3C) and CSV aptamer (ZY5C) for specific marker identification (Song et al., 2013; Zheng et al., 2020) has been widely utilized in CTCs capture, demonstrating high efficiency (Wang et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2023). Meanwhile, to maintain the vitality of captured cells, the biocompatibility of the capture interface is strictly required. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a novel type of carbon nanomaterials with excellent biocompatibility and ease of functionalization, attributed to the abundant carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on their surface (Liu et al., 2023; Ren et al., 2023). These functional groups provide optimal conditions for binding with recognition elements such as aptamers, antibodies, and metal ions, making CQDs suitable for the immunoaffinity isolation of rare CTCs.

Currently, the analysis of PD-L1 expression primarily involves the use of PD-L1 antibodies for IHC of tumor tissues, as well as various kits, including 28-8, 22C3, and SP263 (Tsao et al., 2018). However, there is no standardized protocol for selecting antibodies to detect PD-L1 expression on CTCs, which complicates the standardization of testing technologies and processes. Researchers have used reverse transcription-polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR) to detect PD-L1 mRNA expression in CTCs and have calculated the positive rates based on this method (Bao et al., 2018). Nevertheless, significant challenges remain, including large operational errors, complex procedures, and low reproducibility of results (Feng et al., 2022). Therefore, there is an urgent need for a simpler and more accurate method to analyze PD-L1 expression on CTCs.

Electrochemical sensing technique is characterized by simple equipment, convenient operation, short response time, and high sensitivity (Hu et al., 2023; Shi et al., 2023). However, traditional immunoassays often require externally added reporter molecules (substrate of enzyme or DNAzyme, etc) for signal output or amplification after recognition, which may not be compatible with continuous or real-time sensing. Economically, reagent-free sensors are particularly advantageous, as they reduce long-term operational costs by eliminating the need for reagents and consumables (Nandhakumar et al., 2024). For electrochemical aptamer sensors (E-AB), analyte detection is commonly achieved by immobilizing redox signal reporter-coupled aptamer molecules onto electrodes, which allows for the reversible and real-time transduction of aptamer-target bindings into an electrically measurable signal (Lin et al., 2022; Parrilla et al., 2019). Alternatively, metal nanoparticles (Au or Ag) serve as universal redox-reporters with excellent redox activity, exhibiting rapid electron transfer rate and rich functional groups. This enables the direct generation of detectable electrochemical signals for labeling without the necessity for additional reagents. In this study, we proposed a more accurate strategy to evaluate the efficacy of immunotherapy in NSCLC patients by integrating CTCs enumeration and PD-L1 expression on these CTCs. To achieve this objective, we initially prepared an EpCAM and Vimentin dual aptamer modified nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (E/V-apt-N-CQDs) probe for capturing heterogenous CTCs from NSCLC patients. Concurrently, a biotin-labeled hairpin PD-L1 aptamer (MJ5CP), which has been previously reported to effectively recognize and bind to PD-L1 (Li et al., 2022c), was coupled with gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) to create an electrochemically encoded responsive nanolabel (PD-L1-apt-AuNPs). As illustrated in Scheme 1 , in the presence of PD-L1 protein, the strong and specific recognition between PD-L1 and its hairpin aptamer on the probe resulted in the opening of the hairpin, leading to a conformational change that exposes the biotin site. For the subsequent detection, we engineered robust Fe3O4 and streptavidin (SA) coatings onto bare screen-printed carbon electrode (SPCE) through simple dropping steps to create an effective biosensor substrate. This process uniquely transformed the surface of the SPCE into a magnetic and functional interface, facilitating the strong immobilization of AuNPs through biotin-SA interaction. Using square wave voltammetry (SWV), we enabled qualitative and quantitative detection of PD-L1 expression on CTCs in a reagent-free manner. Finally, the feasibility of this integrated strategy was validated using clinical blood samples from 41 NSCLC patients. We systematically analyzed the relationship between CTC numbers and PD-L1 expression with clinical data. Additionally, we investigated the dynamic changes in CTC numbers and PD-L1 expression were examined in patients receiving immunotherapy.

Streptavidin, HAuCl4•3H2O, sodium citrate, EDC, NHS and urea were all obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Glutaraldehyde was purchased from Shanghai McLean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). PD-L1 antibody was bought from Invitrogen (Carlsbad, CA, USA). Recombinant human E-cadherin protein, recombinant human N-cadherin protein, recombinant human N-cadherin protein, and antibodies of CK19, CD45, EpCAM, and CSV were supplied by Proteintech (Wuhan, China). Recombinant human PD-L1 protein (HEK293) was purchased from MedChemExpress (Monmouth Junction, NJ, USA). Recombinant human EpCAM protein and recombinant human Vimentin protein were purchased from Abcam (Cambridge, MA, USA). Sulfhydrating modified PD-L1 hairpin aptamer, amino-modified EpCAM and CSV aptamers, Cy3-labeled EpCAM and FAM-labeled CSV aptamer were all supplied by Shanghai Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). The sequences of EpCAM, CSV, and PD-L1 hairpin aptamers were 5′-NH2-C6-CACTACAGAGGTTGCGTCTGTCCCACGTTGTCATGGGGGGTT GGCCTG-3' (Li et al., 2022b), 5′-NH2-C6-CACGCATAGCCTTTGCTCCTCGTCTGGAACGTC GCAGCTTTAGTTCTGGGCCTATGCGTG-3' (Zheng et al., 2020), and 5′-HS-SHC6-TACAGGTTCTGGGGGGTGGGTGGGGAACCTGTTAACAGGTTCCCC-3′-Biotin-TEG (Li et al., 2022c), respectively. 1 × PBS (0.01 M, pH 7.2–7.4) was from Nanjing Shenghang Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Jiangsu, China). RPMI-1640 medium (with double antibiotics) were from Jiangsu Keqi Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Jiangsu, China).

All the electrochemical experiments were performed on CHI 660e electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument, CHI, China). The Zeta potential was determined using a Malvern Zeta Sizer Nano-ZS instrument. Transmission electron microscopy images (TEM) were recorded with a FEI-TECNAI G2 transmission electron microscope operating at 200 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were taken on an ESCA Lab MKII X-ray photoelectron spectrometer using Mg Kα radiation. Fourier infrared spectra were collected on Nicolet 460 spectroscopy (Nicolet Corporation, USA). The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) and fluorescence spectra were measured on UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-3100, Shanghai Mapped Instrument Co., Ltd) and fluorescence spectrophotometer (Cary Eclipse G9800A, Agilent Technology Co., Ltd), respectively. Fluorescent microscopy images were taken on Olympus IX73 microscope. Flow cytometry analysis was performed with Agilent NovoCyte flow cytometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Capture of CTCs with differential PD-L1 expression by E/C-apt-N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Suspensions of H1299, A549, and THP-1 cells were collected and counted separately. They were diluted to the desired concentration using PBS solution (pH 7.4). Subsequently, 50 μL of E/C-apt-N-CQDs probe was added to 1.0 mL of cell solution and incubated at room temperature in the dark on a shaker for 30 min. Following incubation, the mixture was centrifuged at 800 r/min for 3 min, after which the cell pellet was re-suspended in PBS. The sample was then excited at 360 nm under an inverted fluorescence microscope, allowing for the observation and counting of cells exhibiting blue fluorescence of the E/C-apt-N-CQDs probe. The capacity of the probe to capture CTCs was evaluated by calculating the capture efficiency using the formula: Capture efficiency = A/B × 100%, where A represented the number of cells labeled with blue fluorescence of the probe, and B represented the total number of spiked cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical detection of recombinant human PD-L1 protein
ce:section.ce:para: The recombinant human PD-L1 protein was initially diluted to gradient concentrations of 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10 ng/mL. Subsequently, 10 μL of each protein solution was thoroughly mixed with 100 μL of the PD-L1-apt-AuNPs probe and incubated at room temperature on a shaker for 30 min. Following incubation, the mixture was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 15 min, after which the supernatant was discarded. The precipitate was then resuspended in 10 μL of PBS (pH 7.4). Next, the resuspended solution was dropped onto the Fe3O4-SA/SPCE. After allowing the sample to dry at room temperature and performing repeated washings, 100 μL of PBS (pH 7.4) was added dropwise to the electrode reaction area and the electrochemical detection was performed. The pretreatment oxidation of AuNPs was conducted at 0.7 V for 60 s using chronoamperometry. Following this, SWV signals were collected in the voltage range of 0.9–0.0 V (step potential = 25 mV, frequency = 15 Hz). The quantification of recombinant PD-L1 protein was based on the peak current of AuNPs around 0.7 V vs SCE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of PD-L1 expression on various cells
ce:section.ce:para: After counting, varying numbers of H1299, A549, or THP-1 cells were diluted and collected via centrifugation. Subsequently, 10 μL of RIPA cell lysate was added (Dai et al., 2025; Zhou et al., 2016), and the cells were lysed on ice for 10 min. Following this, the samples were centrifuged at 4 °C for 15 min at 12,000 r/min, and 10 μL of the cell lysis supernatant was incubated with 100 μL of PD-L1-apt-AuNPs at room temperature for 30 min. The product was then centrifuged at 12,000 r/min for 15 min, and 10 μL of PBS was added to re-suspend the precipitate. The resulting suspension was subsequently applied to the Fe3O4-SA/SPCE and allowed to dry at room temperature. The expression of PD-L1 on cells was then electrochemically detected according to the above protocol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CTCs capture and PD-L1 detection from bloods of NSCLC patients
ce:section.ce:para: A total of 81 participants were enrolled from August 2023 to May 2024 at Xuzhou Central Hospital and the Affiliated Hospital of Xuzhou Medical University. The cohort included 20 healthy volunteers, 20 patients with benign lung nodules, and 41 patients diagnosed with NSCLC. All experiments were conducted in strict accordance with guidelines in the "Interim Rules for Ethical Review of Biomedical Research Involving Human Subjects", as established by the National Health Commission of the People's Republic of China, and in compliance with the relevant provisions of the Helsinki Declaration. This study was approved by the Ethics Committees of Xuzhou Central Hospital (approval number: XZXY-LK-20230610-091) and the Affiliated Hospital of Xuzhou Medical University (approval number: XYFY2024-KL548-01). All participants were provided informed consent before their participation. Whole blood samples were collected in EDTA-K2 anticoagulant tubes and processed within 6 h of collection. The prepared E/C-apt-N-CQDs probe was utilized to capture CTCs from these blood samples. After capture, the expression of PD-L1 on the CTCs was analyzed according to the electrochemical protocols described above.

All experimental results are presented as mean ± SD, obtained from at least three independent experiments. Differences between groups were evaluated using Student's t-test or one-way ANOVA followed by Dunnett's post hoc test. Statistical analyses were performed using SPSS 19.0 software (SPSS, Inc., Chicago, IL). Significance differences are represented as ∗P < 0.05 and ∗∗P < 0.01.

To confirm the successful preparation of N-CQDs, their morphological structure was observed using TEM and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) following separation and purification. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the N-CQDs exhibited a regular spherical structure with good dispersion. The HR-TEM image in Fig. 1B displayed high crystallinity with a lattice stripe of 0.21 nm with face spacing consistent with the (100) face spacing of graphite (Liu et al., 2023). Statistical analysis of their particle sizes revealed an average particle size of (4.47 ± 0.85) nm (Fig. 1C), which aligns with literature reports (Magdy et al., 2023). The surface groups of N-CQDs were analyzed using Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). Fig. S1 identified stretching vibrations of O-H at 3444 cm−1, C=O at 1593 cm−1, indicating the presence of -COOH in N-CQDs. Additional peaks at 1357 cm−1 and 2113 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of aromatic C-N, whereas the peak at 1083 cm−1 corresponded to C-O stretching vibration, which suggested the presence of alcohol hydroxyl groups (Hou et al., 2023). The full X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum in Fig. 1D demonstrated the presence of C 1s, N 1s and O 1s peaks in N-CQDs. High-resolution XPS spectra revealed that the C 1s peak was deconvoluted into two subpeaks at 285.2 eV (C-C/C=C) and 287.7 eV (C-N/C-O) (Fig. 1E). Meanwhile, the main peaks for N 1s and O 1s were located at 399.16 eV (C-N-C) and 531.84 eV (C=O), respectively (Fig. 1F and G), indicating the existence of abundant carboxyl groups in N-CQDs (Yu et al., 2018).

The optical properties of N-CQDs were investigated using fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectroscopy. As presented in Fig. 1H, the N-CQDs exhibited two distinct absorption peaks at 208 nm and 277 nm. These peaks can be attributed to the π - π∗ electron transition of sp 2 conjugated C=C bonds in the aromatic domain and the n - π∗ electron transition (Duan et al., 2023). Furthermore, according to Figure S2 and Fig. 1I, the N-CQDs showed wavelength-dependent fluorescence behavior resulting from variations in surface states. The optimal emission wavelength was identified at 390 nm, while the optimal excitation wavelength was at 325 nm, allowing the N-CQDs to emit bright blue fluorescence under these conditions.

Following the synthesis of N-CQDs, the surface carboxyl groups were activated and subsequently coupled with amino-modified EpCAM and CSV aptamers to prepare the E/C-apt-N-CQDs probe for CTCs capture. To validate the successful conjugation of N-CQDs with the dual aptamers, the aptamers were respectively labeled with CY3 and FAM fluorescein and then co-incubated with N-CQDs. Fluorescence imaging confirmed that, compared to the N-CQDs, the E/C-apt-N-CQDs probe exhibited distinct red fluorescence from CY3 and green fluorescence from FAM. These two fluorescent labels were well co-localized as yellow fluorescence, indicating successful modification of the dual aptamers onto the N-CQDs (Fig. 2 A). Zeta potential analysis, which characterizes the surface charge of particles to verify surface modifications, was conducted before and after the modification. The results showed a notable shift in the zeta potential of the N-CQDs from (−14.21 ± 0.43) mV to (−23.69 ± 0.97) mV (n = 3) after modification, indicating successful attachment of the negatively charged aptamers (Fig. 2B). Furthermore, titration experiments demonstrated that approximately 0.49 mol of EpCAM aptamer and 0.41 mol of CSV aptamer were conjugated to 500 μL of N-CQDs, as calculated by analyzing the amount of unconjugated aptamers remaining in the supernatant (Fig. S3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of capturing CTCs by E/C-apt-N-CQDs probe
ce:section.ce:para: Before CTCs capture, we performed western blot analysis to investigate the differential expression of EpCAM, CSV, and PD-L1 proteins in various human NSCLC cell lines (H1299, A549), human monocytic leukemia cells (THP-1), and peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMC) obtained from healthy volunteers. As revealed in Fig. 2C, extremely low expressions of these three proteins were detected in either THP-1 or PBMC, indicating the potential use of these cell types as negative controls. Furthermore, the expression levels of EpCAM, CSV, and PD-L1 proteins were significantly higher in H1299 cells compared to A549 cells. Additionally, real-time PCR results presented in Fig. 2D further demonstrated that the mRNA level of PD-L1 in H1299 cells was significantly higher than that in A549 cells, which was consistent with previous reports. Therefore, for the subsequent experiments, we chose H1299 cells to represent CTCs with high PD-L1 expression, A549 cells to represent CTCs with low PD-L1 expression, and THP-1 cells as the negative control.

Using H1299 cells as an example, we conducted cell capture by incubating the prepared E/C-apt-N-CQDs probe with H1299 cells for a designated period. Subsequently, the interaction between the cells and the E/C-apt-N-CQDs probe was analyzed using an inverted fluorescence microscope. As shown in Fig. 3 A, H1299 cells exhibited a spherical morphology in suspension, surrounded by bright blue fluorescence of the E/C-apt-N-CQDs probe, effectively outlining the cell shape. To further assess the binding capability of E/C-apt-N-CQDs with CTCs, we measured and compared the fluorescence intensities of H1299 and THP-1 cells following incubation with culture medium (control), N-CQDs, and E/C-apt-N-CQDs using flow cytometry. Fig. 3B revealed that the fluorescence intensity of H1299 cells did not significantly change after incubation with N-CQDs (green curve), indicating that the absence of aptamers on N-CQDs hindered CTC capture. In contrast, a substantial increase in fluorescence was observed when H1299 cells were incubated with the E/C-apt-N-CQDs probe (blue curve), demonstrating that E/C-apt-N-CQDs probe specifically recognized and bound to H1299 cells. Conversely, for THP-1 cells, the fluorescence intensity remained nearly unchanged, regardless of whether N-CQDs were modified with aptamers. Moreover, the E/C-apt-N-CQDs probe demonstrated a significantly higher enrichment efficiency for H1299 and A549 cells compared to THP-1 or PBMC (Fig. 3C). These findings confirmed that the E/C-apt-N-CQDs probe effectively enriched CTCs while minimizing non-specific binding to blood cells that expressed low levels of EpCAM or CSV.

Next, to further clarify the advantages of dual aptamer modifications for enhanced cell capture, we incubated H1299, A549, and THP-1 cells separately with N-CQDs, E-apt-N-CQDs, C-apt-N-CQDs, and E/C-apt-N-CQDs probes under the same conditions, followed by a comparison of their capture efficiencies. As shown in Fig. 3D, none of the probes successfully captured THP-1 cells, mainly due to the lack of specific proteins on the surface of THP-1 cells that could react with the probes. The capture efficiencies for H1299 cells were (13.7 ± 1.1) % for N-CQDs, (63.9 ± 0.2) % for E-apt-N-CQDs, (75.3 ± 0.2) % for C-apt-N-CQDs, and (92.5 ± 0.7) % for E/C-apt-N-CQDs. While the efficiencies for A549 cells were (17.9 ± 1.5) %, (70.6 ± 0.3) %, (59.9 ± 0.3) %, and (94.4 ± 0.5) %, respectively. These results clearly demonstrated that the probe modified with dual aptamer significantly improved capture efficiency compared to probes modified with single EpCAM or CSV aptamers. This improvement is crucial for capturing heterogeneous CTCs and minimizes the risk of false negatives for CTCs with varying phenotypes, thereby facilitating a more comprehensive profile of CTCs in blood.

The capture conditions were optimized, including incubation time between the E/C-apt-N-CQDs probe and CTCs, as well as the incubation temperature (Fig. S4). Under these optimized conditions, as presented in Fig. 3E, the E/C-apt-N-CQDs probe demonstrated exceptional capture capabilities with efficiencies exceeding 90% as the concentration of H1299 cells increased from 5 to 1000 cells/mL. Notably, when the cell concentration was 10 cells/mL or fewer, the capture efficiency reached 100%. Subsequently, we investigated the capture ability of the E/C-apt-N-CQDs probe for H1299 cells at concentrations ranging from 5–500 cells/mL across three different mediums: PBS (pH 7.4), THP-1 cell mixture (106 cells/mL), and blood. As shown in Fig. 3F, the E/C-apt-N-CQDs probe demonstrated exceptional capture capabilities across the three different mediums as the number of H1299 cells increased. The number of H1299 cells captured by the E/C-apt-N-CQDs exhibited a linear relationship with increasing cell concentrations (Fig. 3G). The linear regression equations for PBS, THP-1 cell mixture, and blood were Y = 0.960 X (R 2 = 0.998), Y = 0.922 X (R 2 = 0.999), and Y = 0.905 X (R 2 = 0.999), respectively. These three regression lines basically overlapped with each other, indicating no significant difference in the capture performance of the E/C-apt-N-CQDs probe across the three mediums, which provided a robust foundation for the subsequent specific capture and analysis of CTCs in blood samples from NSCLC patients.

Following this, the captured CTCs were identified using ICC (Fig. S5). Moreover, the heterogeneity of CTCs presented challenges for their applications in clinical settings (Allen, 2024). Fluorescent images of patient-derived CTCs captured by the E/C-apt-N-CQDs probe confirmed its capability to capture different CTC phenotypes in real samples (Fig. S6). To confirm whether the captured CTCs retained high viability for subsequent electrochemical analysis, we systematically evaluated the biocompatibility of the capture probe and the re-culture performance of the CTCs post-capture (Figs. S7–S8). Additionally, CTCs captured from blood samples of one NSCLC patient were stained with Calcein AM and PI to evaluate their viability. As shown in Fig. S9, the majority of the captured CTCs remained viable, indicating that the E/C-apt-N-CQDs probe exhibited excellent biocompatibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PD-L1 analysis on CTCs by PD-L1-apt-AuNPs probe and Fe3O4-SA/SPCE
ce:section.ce:para: For PD-L1 analysis, an electrochemical biosensor, Fe3O4-SA/SPCE, was developed through the successive modification of magnetic Fe3O4 particles and SA onto a commercial SPCE. Among them, Fe3O4 was primarily for easy separation, while SA was used to fix the PD-L1-apt-AuNPs probe onto electrode through SA-biotin interaction, thereby generating the electrochemical signal of AuNPs. The preparation of the PD-L1-apt-AuNPs probe was characterized by TEM, UV–vis, and fluorescence imaging (Figs. S10–S11). The fabrication of Fe3O4-SA/SPCE was traced using CV technique in 1 mM K3Fe(CN)6 solution. As illustrated in Fig. 4 A, the bare SPCE displayed an obvious pair of reversible redox peaks attributed to the redox process of Fe3+/Fe2+ in K3Fe(CN)6 solution. However, following each step of modification, there was a noticeable decrease in current values along with an increase in peak-to-peak separations (ΔE p), which was caused by the poor conductivity of Fe3O4 and SA. Then, the feasibility of the PD-L1-apt-AuNPs probe and Fe3O4-SA/SPCE sensor for PD-L1 detection was first validated using human recombinant PD-L1 protein. As shown in Fig. 4B, there was no obvious oxidation-reduction peak observed on either Fe3O4-SA/SPCE or bare SPCE at a concentration of 10 ng/mL PD-L1. However, following incubation of 10 ng/mL PD-L1 with the PD-L1-apt-AuNPs probe and the subsequent drop-coating of the reaction solution onto Fe3O4-SA/SPCE, a clear reduction peak around +0.7 V appeared in the SWV curve, indicating the reduction of AuNPs on the electrode. These results verified the detection principle for PD-L1: the nucleic acid chain of PD-L1 aptamer is a hairpin structure, with -SH and biotin modification at its 5′- and 3′- ends for conjugation with AuNPs and SA, respectively. In the absence of PD-L1, the hairpin structure of the aptamer maintains the biotin groups in a concealed position, thus preventing AuNPs from binding to Fe3O4-SA/SPCE. When PD-L1 is incubated with the PD-L1-apt-AuNPs probe, the specific recognition between PD-L1 and the aptamer induces the opening of the aptamer's hairpin structure, exposing the biotin groups. When this complex is subsequently drop-casted onto Fe3O4-SA/SPCE, the specific SA-biotin interaction allows for the capture of AuNPs onto the electrode. By SWV detection, the reduction current of AuNPs around 0.7 V could be monitored. Fig. 4C indicated that as the concentration of PD-L1 protein increased from 2–10 ng/mL, the electrochemical responses of AuNPs also remarkably increased. The peak currents of AuNPs around 0.7 V (ΔI) exhibited a good linear relationship with the concentration of recombinant PD-L1 proteins, with the linear regression equation of ΔI (μA) = 0.0518 C (ng/mL) + 0.016 (R 2 = 0.997) (Fig. 4D). These results indicated that the PD-L1-apt-AuNPs probe and Fe3O4-SA/SPCE sensor effectively detected recombinant PD-L1, as expected.

To further assess the specific recognition of PD-L1 by the PD-L1-apt-AuNPs probe in this study, we evaluated the electrochemical responses of several commonly expressed proteins on CTCs, including EpCAM, Vimentin, E-cadherin, N-cadherin, and P-cadherin. As shown in Fig. 4E, a distinct electrochemical response was observed for PD-L1, while no significant electrochemical responses were detected for the aforementioned proteins on the Fe3O4-SA/SPCE. Then, we assessed the stability of the electrochemical assay for PD-L1. The freshly prepared PD-L1-apt-AuNPs probe was incubated with PD-L1 and then detected using the newly fabricated Fe3O4-SA/SPCE sensor. After that, the formed complex on electrode was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C and the current response was measured every two days. Over the course of 8 days of testing, it was observed that the signal declined less than 10% (8.9%), indicating sufficient stability. Moreover, we evaluated reproducibility by preparing one Fe3O4-SA/SPCE sensor and PD-L1-apt-AuNPs probe each day and then detecting PD-L1 in the same way for six consecutive days. Repeatability was achieved by parallelly preparing six probes and sensors in one day and detecting under the same conditions. The reproducibility and repeatability were expressed by calculating the intra- and inter-day precisions, yielding the relative standard deviations (RSDs) of 4.96% and 2.56%, respectively.

Based on the above test results of PD-L1 recombinant protein, we further detected PD-L1 expressions on different cells using the PD-L1-apt-AuNPs probe and Fe3O4-SA/SPCE sensor. Fig. 4F displayed the SWV curves obtained on different number of H1299 cells. The results indicated that all tested H1299 cells exhibited similar electrochemical behaviors, characterized by an obvious AuNPs reduction peak located around +0.7 V. This finding suggested that the PD-L1 expression in different number of H1299 cells can be effectively detected using the PD-L1-apt-AuNPs probe and Fe3O4-SA/SPCE. Additionally, although A549 cells at the same number displayed similar electrochemical responses to H1299 cells, the overall response was significantly lower (Fig. 4G). This implied that PD-L1 expression in H1299 cells was higher than in A549 cells, which is consistent with the above findings obtained from western blot and real-time PCR analyses (Fig. 2). Then, the linear relationship curves between I p and the number of H1299 and A549 cells were plotted. As shown in Fig. 4H, excellent linear relationships were established between I p and cell numbers for both H1299 and A549 cells in the range of 0–500. The corresponding linear regression equations were I p (H1299) = 0.0083 C + 0.21 (R 2 = 0.996) and I p (A549) = 0.0046 C + 0.12 (R 2 = 0.997), respectively. To evaluate the specificity of the electrochemical assay for PD-L1, we used THP-1 cells, which do not express PD-L1, as a negative control. As displayed in Fig. 4I, after incubating 500 THP-1 cells with the PD-L1-apt-AuNPs probe, no obvious electrochemical response corresponding to PD-L1 protein was detected on Fe3O4-SA/SPCE. Therefore, based on comparative analysis of H1299, A549, and THP-1 cells, we concluded that the PD-L1-apt-AuNPs probe and Fe3O4-SA/SPCE sensor were able to sensitively and specifically detecting varying levels of PD-L1 expression across different cell types. This capability enhances their potential application in analyzing PD-L1 expression on CTCs from clinical patients.

Given the exceptional capture ability of E/C-apt-N-CQDs and the effective PD-L1 detection capability of the PD-L1-apt-AuNPs probe, peripheral blood samples were collected from NSCLC patients to assess their potential application. Initially, 1.0 mL of fresh blood samples from a cohort of 33 NSCLC patients before treatment were included (Table S1), and subsequent follow-up aimed to investigate the correlation between CTC numbers and clinical data. The analysis revealed that CTC counts were not associated with gender (Fig. S12A), age (Fig. S12B), clinical subtypes (Fig. S12C), or lymph node metastasis (Fig. S12E) of NSCLC patients. Nevertheless, patients in advanced clinical stages (Ⅲ and Ⅳ) exhibited significantly higher CTC numbers than those in early clinical stage (Ⅰ) (Fig. S12D). Moreover, patients with distant metastasis (M1) displayed significantly elevated CTC levels compared to those without distant metastasis (M0) (Fig. S12F). These results indicated a strong association between high CTC numbers and clinical progression. Additionally, blood samples were collected from 20 patients with benign lung nodules (Table S2) and 20 healthy volunteers (Table S3). Notably, no CTCs were detected in either patients with benign lung nodules or healthy volunteers, in contrast to NSCLC patients (Fig. S12G), suggesting the absence of NSCLC in these individuals (CTC number >1 indicating a positive result). These findings illustrated the potential of E/C-apt-N-CQDs for CTCs detection in NSCLC patients and for monitoring disease progression.

Furthermore, PD-L1 expression in CTCs obtained from NSCLC patients was assessed using the PD-L1-apt-AuNPs probe. The electrochemical curve corresponding to PD-L1 expression in CTCs from patient #8 was illustrated in Fig. 5 A. Subsequently, ICC assay was conducted to validate the PD-L1 expression state. As shown in Fig. 5B, three CTCs were detected from patient #8, all of which exhibited PD-L1 expression. Additionally, PD-L1 levels in CTCs were not significantly associated with gender, age, clinical subtypes, clinical stages, or lymph node metastasis of NSCLC patients (Fig. 5C–G). However, patients with distant metastasis (M1) displayed significantly lower PD-L1 levels in CTCs compared to those without distant metastasis (M0) (Fig. 5H), indicating a notable association between low PD-L1 levels and clinical progression. These results demonstrated that the PD-L1-apt-AuNPs probe was effective in identifying PD-L1 expression in CTCs captured by E/C-apt-N-CQDs.

Then, a cohort of 8 NSCLC patients undergoing PD-L1/PD-1 inhibitor therapy was recruited (Table S4), and a follow-up was conducted to observe variations in CTC counts and PD-L1 expression. Peripheral blood samples (1.0 mL) were collected from each patient both before and two weeks after ICIs treatment. Following treatment, CTC counts decreased in 6 patients while increased in 2 patients, indicating a general trend of decreased CTC numbers (Fig. 5I) and suggesting the potential efficacy of ICIs for most patients. Notably, PD-L1 levels decreased in 5 patients but increased in 3 patients. However, no significant changes in PD-L1 expression on CTCs were observed two weeks after ICIs treatment (Fig. 5J). PD-L1, a critical biomarker for PD-L1/PD-1 inhibitors, can be utilized in liquid biopsies for predicting the outcomes of immunotherapy in NSCLC (Duchemann et al., 2021). Monitoring PD-L1 expression on CTCs in NSCLC patients treated with pembrolizumab could potentially predict treatment efficacy. Patients with progressive disease more frequently exhibited increased total and low PD-L1 CTCs after the first therapy cycle compared to those who experienced clinical benefit (Spiliotaki et al., 2023). Interestingly, in our study, an increase in CTC numbers coupled with decreased PD-L1 expression was observed in 25.0% of patients (2/8), indicating a possible lack of benefit for these individuals. Conversely, a decrease in CTC numbers, along with increased PD-L1 expression, was observed in 37.5% of patients (3/8) two weeks after ICIs treatment, suggesting possible clinical benefits for these patients in the future (Table S4). Due to the limited sample size, the short duration of follow-up, and the absence of treatment response outcomes, we are unable to draw definitive conclusions at this stage. However, these results highlight the dynamic detection capabilities of the E/V-apt-N-CQDs and PD-L1-apt-AuNPs for measuring CTC counts and PD-L1 levels in NSCLC patients undergoing ICIs therapy. This underscores the potential clinical application of the liquid biopsy platform developed in this study. Therefore, a more comprehensive investigation is required to explore the relationship between dynamic changes in CTC numbers, as well as PD-L1 expression and treatment outcomes.

Finally, we compared our assay with existing clinical methods for PD-L1. As summarized in Table S5, although tissue-based PD-L1 IHC assays have received the most FDA approvals as companion diagnostics for immunotherapies, they rely on tumor tissue collected at the time of diagnosis. This reliance presents challenges related to tissue availability and biological variability, particularly given that protein expression can fluctuate throughout the course of treatment (Lim et al., 2016). Furthermore, the lack of standardized protocols among various IHC PD-L1 tests results in significant discrepancies in PD-L1 expression cutoffs and testing standards across different antibody clones and devices (Chen et al., 2019). In this study, we present the E/C-apt-N-CQDs and PD-L1-Apt-AuNPs probe, which addresses the above noted challenges associated with tissue-based testing. This liquid assay effectively tracks dynamic changes in CTC counts and PD-L1 expression in NSCLC patients with high sensitivity and specificity, thereby providing valuable insights for personalized treatment strategies.

In conclusion, we developed a liquid biopsy platform that integrated high-performance E/C-apt-N-CQDs as the capture probe with reagent-less electrochemical detection of PD-L1. The engineered capture probe demonstrated high efficiency in recognizing and capturing a limited number of CTCs with high viability, which is essential for the subsequent detection of PD-L1 protein expression. Moreover, the electrochemical sensor, labeled with nano-gold, exhibited a rapid response to low concentrations of PD-L1, achieving remarkable sensitivity and selectivity, capable of detecting as low as 2 ng/mL PD-L1 without the need for additional reagents. The efficacy of this approach was thoroughly assessed through experiments involving recombinant proteins, cells, and clinical patient samples. Overall, the simultaneous counting of rare CTCs and analysis of PD-L1 expression on these cells from the blood samples of NSCLC patients provide important insights for personalized immunotherapy, thereby facilitating the application of companion tests for PD-L1 expression.
